one_line_takeaway: ATBench is a 1,000-trajectory synthetic benchmark for long-horizon agent safety with a delayed-trigger protocol and a 3-axis (source/failure/harm) diagnostic taxonomy; frontier LLMs can flag unsafe trajectories (~77% F1) but almost entirely fail to attribute the underlying cause (~13-34% fine-grained), especially for tool-mediated risks.
key_finding: ATBench is far from saturated even for frontier models, and fine-grained causal diagnosis is dramatically harder than binary detection. On coarse binary classification the best F1 is GPT-5.4 at 76.7%, followed by Gemini-3.1-Pro (75.0%) and the trajectory-trained AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (71.1%). On fine-grained diagnosis, even strong closed models collapse: GPT-5.4 reaches only 33.6% (risk source), 13.5% (failure mode), and 30.2% (harm). The trajectory-trained AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B is strongest on fine-grained labels (46.8% / 16.5% / 40.6%). Off-the-shelf guard models are weak on the coarse task (LlamaGuard3-8B 7.3% F1, Qwen3-Guard 0.8% F1, LlamaGuard4-12B 41.7% F1). Cross-benchmark comparison shows most models drop sharply on ATBench versus prior benchmarks (e.g., GPT-5.2 90.8% on R-Judge vs 69.0% here; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B 91.8% vs 64.0%). Difficulty is concentrated in tool-mediated leaf categories: 'Malicious Tool Execution' is near zero for three of four evaluators (24.0% best), and 'Choosing Malicious Tool' reaches only 5.6-22.2%, while explicit user-driven risks (Malicious User/Jailbreak: 73.8% GPT-5.4, 83.6% Qwen3.5-397B) are comparatively tractable.
core_contribution: ATBench is a trajectory-level benchmark for evaluating LLM-agent safety over long-horizon, tool-mediated interactions. Its stated goal is 'maximize diversity while preserving realism.' The paper's own contributions are: (1) a released benchmark of 1,000 held-out multi-turn agent trajectories (503 safe / 497 unsafe) built on a heterogeneous tool pool (2,084 available tools, 1,954 actually invoked), averaging 9.01 turns and 3.95k tokens; (2) a three-dimensional agentic-safety taxonomy over risk source (8 leaf categories), failure mode (14 leaf categories), and real-world harm (10 leaf categories) used both as a construction scaffold and as a fine-grained diagnostic label space; (3) a taxonomy-guided data-generation engine combining risk sampling, planner-based trajectory synthesis, paired safe/unsafe construction, and a 'setup-exploit' delayed-trigger long-context protocol that plants a risk early and realizes it later; and (4) an empirical study showing the benchmark is harder than prior trajectory benchmarks and supports category-stratified diagnosis. Note on attribution: the appendix states the three-dimensional taxonomy is organized 'Following AgentDoG' (Liu et al., 2026, a prior paper from the same group and one of the evaluated guard models), so the taxonomy is adapted rather than wholly novel; the delayed-trigger generation engine and the released 1,000-trajectory benchmark are the paper's clearer original contributions.
threat_model: The benchmark models safety risks that emerge across a full agent trajectory rather than from a single prompt. Risk sources span four families: user input (malicious instruction/jailbreak, direct prompt injection), environmental observation (indirect prompt injection, unreliable/misinformation), external entities/tools (tool-description injection, malicious tool execution, corrupted tool feedback), and internal logic (inherent agent/LLM failures). A central adversarial mechanism is the delayed-trigger protocol: a risk is planted early (e.g., an injected instruction hidden in a tool description or environment observation) and only becomes safety-critical several steps later, testing whether an evaluator can track persistent state and delayed dependencies. The paper is evaluation-focused, not defense-focused; it measures whether general-purpose LLMs and specialized guard models can (a) classify a trajectory as safe/unsafe and (b) recover the fine-grained risk source, failure mode, and harm type.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) each unsafe trajectory carries only one primary label per dimension, potentially missing valid secondary interpretations in multi-causal cases; (2) all trajectories are in English; (3) the benchmark is text-and-tool only, excluding multimodal/embodied settings. Reviewer-noted: (a) the data is fully synthetic (planner + simulators generate tool calls and environment feedback), so 'realism' is asserted via filtering and human audit rather than derived from real agent deployments; (b) the fine-grained taxonomy is adapted from the group's own prior AgentDoG work, and AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (which tops fine-grained diagnosis) is trained on that same taxonomy, so the strongest fine-grained baseline shares construction assumptions with the benchmark; (c) the human audit itself produced 165 fine-grained label corrections across 129 of 497 unsafe trajectories, indicating the automatic label pipeline had a substantial fine-grained error rate before correction.

datasets_used: ATBench (the paper's own benchmark): 1,000 trajectories, 503 safe / 497 unsafe, avg 9.01 turns, avg 3.95k tokens, 2,084 available tools, 1,954 invoked tools
ATBench500 (earlier 500-case release of the same benchmark, used as a comparison point)
R-Judge (prior trajectory benchmark, 564 cases, used for cross-benchmark comparison)
ASSE-Safety (1,476 cases) and ASSE-Security (817 cases) from AgentAuditor/ASSEBench (comparison)
Tool pool adapted from ToolBench (ToolLLM) and ToolAlpaca plus real public APIs (e.g., RapidAPI) and simulated tools
quantitative_results: GPT-5.4: coarse Acc 73.7%, Prec 68.5%, Rec 87.1%, F1 76.7% (best coarse F1); fine-grained R.S. 33.6%, F.M. 13.5%, R.H. 30.2%
Gemini-3.1-Pro: coarse F1 75.0%; fine-grained R.S. 24.8%, F.M. 12.6%, R.H. 18.5%
Gemini-3-Flash: coarse F1 74.9%; R.S. 18.4%, F.M. 8.3%, R.H. 15.0%
AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (trajectory-trained guard): coarse F1 71.1%; best fine-grained R.S. 46.8%, F.M. 16.5%, R.H. 40.6%
Qwen3.5-397B-A17B: coarse F1 67.8%; Qwen3-235B-A22B: coarse F1 60.8%
Guard models coarse F1: ShieldAgent 67.7%, LlamaGuard4-12B 41.7%, LlamaGuard3-8B 7.3%, Qwen3-Guard 0.8%
Cross-benchmark accuracy drop: GPT-5.2 90.8% (R-Judge) -> 69.0% (ATBench); Qwen3-235B 85.1% -> 59.2%; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B 91.8% -> 64.0%

Leaf-category difficulty: Malicious User/Jailbreak 73.8% (GPT-5.4) / 83.6% (Qwen3.5-397B); Malicious Tool Execution ~24.0% best and near zero for others; Choosing Malicious Tool 5.6% (AgentDoG), 16.7% (GPT-5.4), 22.2% (Qwen3.5-397B)
Human audit produced 5 binary corrections and 165 fine-grained corrections across 129 unsafe trajectories
Table 1 deltas vs ATBench500: cases +100.0%, available tools +32.3%, used tools +44.0%, avg turns +0.4%, avg tokens +159.9%
baselines_compared: Open-source: Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, Qwen3.5-4B, Qwen3-4B, QwQ-32B, Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct
Guard models: LlamaGuard3-8B, LlamaGuard4-12B, Qwen3-Guard, ShieldAgent, AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B
